category_title: Students using aggressive/warrior imagery for catharsis
minimal_context: The student is writing a personal blog post about someone they consider to be a survivor during a creative writing session.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please start working on your creative writing blog posts about people you consider to be survivors.

STUDENT: Can we discuss the details of the day's lesson first? I want to check in so I'm not caught off guard.

TUTOR: We are just focusing on writing the blog posts right now. Go ahead and write.

STUDENT: Okay. I'm writing about a survivor. 'You are a warrior who has fought through the very depths of the Earth.'

TUTOR: Keep going. What else are you adding to the post?

STUDENT: (Loudly, with an angry and aggressive tone) 'The screams in the air are thick / I can feel her pain in my lungs!' You are a brave Amazonian warrior!

options.A: Schedule the Speak unit later in the school year, after the students know one another better.
options.B: Encourage the student to complete homework after every session, which involves practicing new writing techniques and completing a journal.
options.C: Assign a sequenced writing project where the student writes four blog posts on the same subject over the course of the semester.
options.D: Implement a three-revision cycle for the blog posts where only the final, third draft is graded.
